subject: Science
chapter: Chapter 2: Is Matter Around Us Pure

key_points: A pure substance has a fixed composition and definite properties
Mixtures contain two or more substances physically combined
Mixtures can be homogeneous (uniform composition) or heterogeneous (non-uniform composition)
Solutions are homogeneous mixtures of solute and solvent
Alloys are homogeneous mixtures of metals or metals with non-metals
Suspensions are heterogeneous mixtures where particles settle on standing
Colloids have particles intermediate in size between solution and suspension
Tyndall effect is scattering of light by colloidal particles
examples: Salt and sugar are pure substances
Air and saltwater are homogeneous mixtures (solutions)
Oil and water form heterogeneous mixtures
Milk is a colloid showing Tyndall effect
Iron in molten state mixed with carbon forms an alloy
formulas: Solubility = Mass of solute / Volume of solvent × 100
Saturated solution: no more solute can dissolve at a given temperature
exam_tips: Identify whether a substance is pure or a mixture in examples
Explain types of mixtures with examples
Remember key terms: solute, solvent, colloid, suspension, alloy
Use Tyndall effect to distinguish between colloid and solution
For numerical problems, check if solution is saturated and calculate solubility
